resume:
Jordan Avery — Customer Support Intern Candidate
Third-year undergraduate, Communications major with a minor in Information Systems. Seeking a customer-support internship where I can apply my service mindset and growing technical curiosity to help real customers.

Objective
I am a people-first, detail-oriented student who genuinely enjoys solving problems for others. Over the past three years I have built a foundation in front-line service through part-time and volunteer roles, and I am eager to translate that into a structured support environment with tooling, queues, and metrics. My goal for this internship is to learn how a modern support organization actually runs day to day, to get comfortable in a ticketing system, and to contribute by clearing real tickets rather than just shadowing.

Education
State University, expected graduation next spring. Relevant coursework: Interpersonal Communication, Business Writing, Introduction to Databases, Human-Computer Interaction, and Statistics for the Social Sciences. GPA is solid and I have made the dean's list in two of the last four semesters. I am also a peer note-taker for the disability services office, which has sharpened my ability to capture information accurately and quickly.

Experience
Campus IT Help Desk — Student Assistant (about 14 months, ongoing). I work roughly fifteen hours a week at the front counter and on the phones, helping students and faculty with password resets, Wi-Fi connectivity, learning-management-system access, and basic printing and email problems. I log every interaction in the help-desk ticketing tool, escalate anything hardware-related to the full-time technicians, and follow up with the requester until the ticket is closed. During the back-to-school rush each semester the queue gets very long and I have learned to triage quickly, group similar issues, and write clear internal notes so the next person can pick up where I left off. I am frequently the first point of contact, which means I set the tone for whether someone walks away frustrated or relieved.

Retail Sales Associate — Coastal Outfitters (two summers). I worked the floor and the returns counter at a busy outdoor-gear store. The returns counter is where I learned the most about customer emotion, because people arrive already annoyed and it is my job to de-escalate, understand what actually went wrong, and find a resolution within store policy. I processed returns and exchanges, handled warranty questions, and helped customers pick replacement gear. I was recognized by my manager for staying calm during a particularly heavy holiday-return week, and I was trusted to train two newer seasonal associates on the point-of-sale system and the return workflow.

Volunteer — Community Tax Assistance Program (one tax season). I greeted visitors, verified that they had the right documents before they sat down with a preparer, and managed the sign-in sheet and waitlist so the floor ran smoothly. This was essentially queue management and intake under time pressure, and it taught me how much smoother an operation runs when intake is clean and expectations are set early.

Student Organization — Service Chair, Communications Club. I coordinated volunteer events, sent reminder communications to members, and tracked attendance. I am comfortable sending clear, friendly written updates to groups of people and keeping a simple tracker current.

Skills
Front-line service and de-escalation. Ticketing and case logging (campus help-desk tool). Clear written communication and internal note-taking. Basic SQL queries from my databases course, enough to read a table and filter rows. Spreadsheet basics including filters, sorting, and simple pivot tables. Comfortable learning new software quickly; I picked up the help-desk tool in about two shifts. Familiar with the idea of common support metrics like first-contact resolution, average handle time, and customer satisfaction, though I have not personally owned a metric target yet.

What I am looking for
I want to work somewhere that takes support seriously as a craft, not as a cost center. I want a manager who will give me a real queue, real feedback, and a chance to see how my handling of a ticket connects to a satisfaction score or a resolution-time number. I am especially interested in learning how macros, knowledge-base articles, and escalation paths are designed, because at the help desk I have seen how a good canned response saves everyone time and a bad one creates a second ticket.

Strengths and growth areas
My strengths are empathy, patience, and clear writing. My growth areas are technical depth — I am still early in my information-systems coursework — and working within formal service-level targets, which I have read about but not yet been measured against. I am honest about that gap and I see this internship as the place to close it.

References available on request. I am able to commit to a full-time summer schedule and a part-time continuation into the fall semester if that is useful to the team.

Additional context
A few specifics that may be relevant to a Tier-1 support role. At the help desk I have written several short internal notes that other student assistants now reuse, which taught me that a clear, well-scoped note saves the whole team time and prevents a second contact on the same issue. I have shadowed a full-time technician through a handful of harder escalations to understand what a clean handoff with reproduction steps looks like from the receiving side, not just the sending side. I am comfortable working a queue with a visible backlog and have learned not to cherry-pick the easy tickets, because that just leaves the hard ones to pile up for someone else. I read documentation carefully before asking, and when I do ask I try to come with what I already tried so the answer is fast. I am genuinely excited to be measured against first-contact resolution and a satisfaction score for the first time, because right now I only have a qualitative sense of whether I did well, and I want the real signal so I can actually improve shift over shift rather than guess.
jd: Brightline Software is hiring a Customer Support Intern for the summer. You will join our Tier-1 support pod, work a real ticket queue in Zendesk under a mentor, and be measured on the same first-contact-resolution and CSAT signals as the full-time team (with a ramp). Responsibilities: respond to inbound email and chat tickets for our SMB customers, follow documented troubleshooting runbooks, write clear internal notes, escalate cleanly to Tier-2 with reproduction steps, and contribute at least two knowledge-base article drafts by the end of the program. We are looking for someone coachable who takes ownership of their tickets, communicates with empathy, and is comfortable being held to a queue and a quality bar. Prior front-line service experience and basic familiarity with a ticketing tool are pluses. This is a hands-on role; you will own outcomes, not just shadow.

history.q: Tell me about your current role at the campus help desk and what you actually handle day to day.
Walk me through how you log and follow up on a ticket from open to close.
Describe a time you de-escalated an upset person at the returns counter.
What do first-contact resolution and CSAT mean to you, and have you been measured on either?
Tell me about a ticket you personally got wrong. What happened?
When you escalate to a full-time technician, what do you include so they can act fast?
How do you triage when the back-to-school queue is overwhelming?
Give me an example of a canned response or note that you improved.
Tell me about a time a customer left unhappy despite your best effort. What was your role in that outcome?
How do you handle being held to a quality bar you don't fully agree with?
What feedback have you gotten from a manager that was hard to hear?
Let's go back to that unhappy customer. Be specific about what you, personally, could have done differently.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly that one wasn't really on me. The student showed up right at closing with a broken laptop, and our policy is that hardware goes to the full-time techs, who had already left for the day. The ticketing tool was also being slow that afternoon so I couldn't even pull up their history fast enough. And the knowledge-base article for that error was outdated, so the steps I followed didn't work. I did everything I was supposed to do — it's just that the system and the staffing weren't set up to help that person, so they left frustrated and that's kind of on the process, not me.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: that's kind of on the process, not me

expected-yield: one concrete thing the candidate personally could have controlled in that closing-time interaction — how they set the customer's expectations, what follow-up they committed to, or what they logged for the morning tech — instead of attributing the bad outcome entirely to staffing, tooling, and the knowledge base
